explanations.5: The condition checks if the number of arguments is NOT equal to 2; the '!' negates the equality test, so this line exits if you didn't provide exactly two arguments.
explanations.13: The '&&' chains commands together so that the next command only runs if the previous one succeeded; here it's used to echo an error and exit only if the source folder doesn't exist.
explanations.16: This command changes your current directory to the source folder, and '>/dev/null' hides the normal output that would show the new directory path.
explanations.18: The '\e[37;1m' and '\e[0;1m' are ANSI color codes that make the text appear in bright white; they're not actually part of the message.
explanations.41: The '<(...)' syntax is called process substitution; it lets you use the output of a command as if it were a file, so diff can compare two command outputs side-by-side.
